items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: What is the product of mild oxidation of a primary alcohol?
items.correct: An aldehyde (which can be further oxidised to a carboxylic acid).
items.misconception: A primary alcohol is oxidised directly to a ketone.
items.random_wrong: A primary alcohol is oxidised to an alkene by losing water.
items.why: Students confuse the products of primary vs secondary alcohol oxidation because they look structurally similar, but only secondary alcohols form ketones.

items.q: Why do tertiary halogenoalkanes typically react via SN1 rather than SN2?
items.correct: The tertiary carbocation intermediate is stabilised by three alkyl groups (positive inductive effect), making the SN1 pathway favourable.
items.misconception: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN1 because they are more reactive in every way.
items.random_wrong: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN2 because they have more bonds to break.
items.why: Students mix up steric and electronic factors — they don't realise it's the STABILITY of the carbocation (electronic) plus STERIC HINDRANCE (blocking SN2) that drives the SN1 preference.

items.q: An alkane has 8 carbons. How many hydrogens? (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂)
items.why: The real figure is 18 H atoms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
